urls.title: AM par Alexandre Mazzia – Marseille - a MICHELIN Guide Restaurant
urls.content:
## AM par Alexandre Mazzia

9 rue François-Rocca, Marseille, 13008, France

€€€€ · Creative

In his eatery near the Stade Vélodrome, this artist-cum-chef on a perpetual creative quest has elevated the small portion to the realm of art. He is a virtuoso when it comes to spices, roasting and smoking and his childhood in Congo flows freely through his cuisine. This distinctive hallmark is further enriched by a unique use of vinegars (over 400 house creations) that add depth and complexity to his jus. The resulting lineup of some forty small dishes, a truly herculean task, is surgically crafted and often visually striking: cuttlefish steamed in sake, puffed barley, briny seasoning and seaweed; watercress, smoked beetroot, langoustine milk, iced burnt langoustine heads. Each meal is a feat of culinary excellence, supervised by a diffident, yet single-minded chef, whose watchful eye never leaves the serving hatch.

AM is the creation of Alexandre Mazzia. The restaurant is tiny, with a kitchen so small that many domestic kitchens are bigger. It contains 24 seats of which there are 5 counter seats where you basically sit right on the pass.  
Mr. Mazzia was born and raised in Congo and claims to be still influenced by his childhood experience.  
The menu is composed differently than in other restaurants as he always has six servings and the diner just chooses how many little plates each serving contains.  
I, of course, went for the jugular and the pricey (€380) biggest menu. This contains 36(!) small dishes grouped together in what seems to be a random order of 6 servings.  
I have been at AM before a few times but for obvious reasons not in the last three years. Mr. Mazzia has not only developed but made a quantum leap. This is so far the meal of the year (276 as of the end of October, not counting any meals in Ryokans where I stayed overnight) and given the advance state of the year, maybe it will stay that way.  
His compositions are bold while at the same time delicate. The layering of flavors is sometimes subtle and sometimes aggressive. The execution is always flawless, which is a feat when serving 36 dishes. But most of all the combination of ingredients and thus flavors is simply stunning. Dishes like Goosefoot in tempura-vodka, pike roe, chili, shell dust or Seaweed, sweet potato ointment, licorice, bottarga sound like they could never work but they do. Very much so.  
Mr. Mazzia does all of this in a tiny little kitchen with three other cooks while chatting with the regulars who are of course placed at the counter. To churn out such a kitchen this number of dishes for 24 dinners is by itself an accomplishment, but doing it at such a quality and tastiness beggars belief.  
The front of the house is very competent in being able to explain all dishes. It is quite an accomplishment to be able to announce what must be hundreds of ingredients during the whole meal.  
There is a champagne, wine, sake and non alcoholic pairing available which gets managed by the team flexible depending on the speed of drinking.  
All together this makes not for a good, but a great meal. I cannot wait to go back. It truly is worth a journey.

urls.title: The Top Michelin Star Restaurants in Marseille - Paris Eater
urls.content:
Marseille is home to some incredibly inventive chefs at some of the best Michelin Star restaurants in France.

Historically Marseille hasn’t been a hot spot for fine dining but in recent years this has changed. There’s been a huge post-covid shift away from the capital and Marseille has certainly benefited from this.

Gone are the days of ancient bistros with zero innovation – nowadays Marseille is a culinary hotspot with a vibrant restaurant scene. It’s not just tapas spots – there’s plenty of great Michelin starred restaurants to explore. Here’s a list of my favourite spots where you won’t be disappointed.

Staying in Marseille and want to know which are the best Michelin star restaurants in the city? Read on to discover my personal recommendations.

### Une Table au Sud

_Une Table au Sud_ is an excellent 1 Michelin star restaurant nestled right next to the old port (with incredible panoramic views to boot). The food, as the name suggest, is pure South of France and my gosh is it good. It’s run by Chef and owner Ludovic Turac (ex. Le Bristol & Guy Savoy) who was born in Marseille and

Like many great restaurants in the area, Une Table au Sud makes maximum use of the incredible regional produce available. Provençal vegetables, meat from the pre-alps and local fish shine on the plate and there’s also plenty of modern technique on show to really show them off.

![une table au sud Marseille](https://www.pariseater.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/09/best-michelin-star-restaurants-marseille-une-table-au-sud-3-683x1024.jpg)

![aioli](https://www.pariseater.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/09/best-michelin-star-restaurants-marseille-une-table-au-sud-4-683x1024.jpg)

Go for lunch and get their _Marseille_ menu. It’s all about local cuisine and produce and is very reasonable for a 1\* restaurant coming in at €125 ex. drinks. For this you’ll get 5 dishes with snacks and pre-desserts included.

It’s seasonal but expect two signature dishes and spoiler: they’re _really_ good. The first is their _aioli_ – a classic dish from the south of France. Slow cooked cod loin is covered in a light, airy garlic mayonnaise sauce and comes with an incredible ‘persillade’ sauce (garlic, parsley and shallots).

![bouillabaisse](https://www.pariseater.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/09/best-michelin-star-restaurants-marseille-une-table-au-sud-2-683x1024.jpg)

![dessert](https://www.pariseater.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/09/best-michelin-star-restaurants-marseille-une-table-au-sud-1-683x1024.jpg)

The second is their version of of a [bouillabaisse](https://www.pariseater.com/france-travel-guides/finding-the-best-bouillabaisse-in-marseille/) soup. Red Mullet is cooked to precision and comes with confit fennel and potato. The ‘soup’ is really a rich sauce and is _incredible_. I really like the fact that they leave the rest of the sauce and the garlic mayonnaise (rouille) on the table for you to serve yourself more.

#### What to know before you visit

*   Closed on Mondays and Sundays
*   You can reserve online

**Address:** 2 Quai du Port, 13002 Marseille  
**Tel:** 04 91 90 63 53  
**Website:** [https://www.unetableausud.com](https://www.unetableausud.com)

### Le Petit Nice Passedat

Le Petit Nice Passedat is 3 Michelin star restaurant and known to local and international foodies as _the_ temple of fish & seafood in Marseille. The restaurant is situated in the main 5\* hotel with the dining room having stunning panoramic views out across the sea.

![Le Petit Nice Passedat garden of seafood](https://www.pariseater.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/06/Le-petit-nice-passedat-sea-food-768x1024.jpg)

![le petit nice passedat Marseille](https://www.pariseater.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/08/restaurants-marseille-le-petit-nice-passedat-inside.jpg)

The food is all about local fish and seafood and it is _incredible_. When I say local, it really is local. The head Chef, Gérald Passedat, is passionate about forgotten rockfish and one of many of he menus revolve around exactly this. The highlight for me are Passedat’s sauces and broths.

![dessert outside](https://www.pariseater.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/08/restaurants-marseille-le-petit-nice-passedat-dessert.jpg)

![Le Petit Nice passed sea bass](https://www.pariseater.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/06/Le-petit-nice-passedat-768x1024.jpg)

Meat jus tends to be the star of the show in most dining restaurant menus but not here. Deep, rich and perfectly balanced fish based sauces pair so well with the expert fish cookery on show. It is on the pricier side with even the 3 course lunch menu coming in at just under €200 but well worth it if you want to enjoy a special meal.

#### What to know before you visit

*   Free valet parking is available in the hotel

**Address**: 7 Rue des Braves Anse de Maldormé, 156 Cor Président John Fitzgerald Kennedy, 13007 Marseille  
**Tel:** 04 91 59 25 92  
**Website**: [https://www.passedat.fr/fr/le-petit-nice/restaurant-3-etoiles-marseille-bord-de-mer](https://www.passedat.fr/fr/le-petit-nice/restaurant-3-etoiles-marseille-bord-de-mer)

### Saisons

**Unfortunately Saisons is now permanently closed.**

Restaurant Saisons is a 1 Michelin star restaurant headed up by Chef Julien Diaz. Only a few steps away from the Place Castellane, it’s an inventive, modern restaurant with a strong focus on Mediterranean cuisine. Chef Diaz, having spent time in London and Corsica, returned to his native Marseille to start Saisons with Sommelier Guillaume Bonneaud.

The modern dining room has industrial accents – wood, steel, iron – yet is surprisingly cosy. On the food side there’s numerous menus to choose from with the cheapest coming in at a very reasonable €40 for 3 courses during week day lunches. The plates are small, punchy and experimental yet unpretentious. As you would imagine, the dishes change with the season and the ingredients are locally sourced. Highlights for me include their seasonal risotto and fregola with cured ham.

### Alcyone

**Unfortunately Alcyone is now permanently closed.**

Started in 2013 by Chef Lionel Levy (trained by Alain Ducasse), Alcyone is a 1 Michelin starred restaurant specialising in high end Mediterranean cuisine. Nestled in the famous Hotel Dieu hotel in the city centre, the dining room has spectacular views across to the iconic Basilica of Notre-Dame of la Garde.

There are two menus to choose from at Alycone with either 5 or 8 courses priced at €110 and €150 respectively. The food is refined, precise and on the more traditional side. There is some element of creativity with their use of spices but the focus is mainly on the incredible local produce. As with many great restaurants in Marseille, their fish courses are dictated by what was landed that day. Expect local rascasse, seabass and red mullet – all incredibly well cooked.

#### What to know before you visit

*   Closes yearly in August for summer vacations
*   You can reserve online

**Address:** InterContinental Marseille – Hotel Dieu, 1 Pl. Daviel, 13002 Marseille  
**Tel:** 04 13 42 43 43  
**Website:** [https://marseille.intercontinental.com/alcyone/](https://marseille.intercontinental.com/alcyone/)

### AM

AM by Alexandre Mazzia is the 2nd of the two restaurants holding a mighty 3 Michelin stars in Marseille. Located to the south of the city in a surprisingly residential district, the restaurant has become legendary with foodies across France.

The chef, having grown up in Congo in Africa, has an incredibly diverse and innovative cuisine on offer. Small influences of his childhood are on show and pepper his plates in a really interesting manner – hints of spice, smoked sauces and proteins – it’s an absolute gem of a restaurant. Combinations such as langoustine, sesame, bonito and lemon surprise the palate and his seaweed popcorn is mind-blowing. His ‘voyages’ (menus) vary in courses but for lunch you can try his smallest menu for €195 although you need to call to book this menu. Absolutely worth a visit if you’re a fan of inventive cuisine!

#### What to know before you visit

*   Open Wednesday – Saturday
*   You can reserve online

**Address:** 9 Rue François Rocca, 13008 Marseille  
**Tel:** 04 91 24 83 63  
**Website:** [https://www.alexandre-mazzia.com](https://www.alexandre-mazzia.com)

# AM par Alexandre Mazzia – Marseille - a MICHELIN Guide Restaurant

## AM par Alexandre Mazzia

9 rue François-Rocca, Marseille, 13008, France

€€€€ · Creative

In his eatery near the Stade Vélodrome, this artist-cum-chef on a perpetual creative quest has elevated the small portion to the realm of art. He is a virtuoso when it comes to spices, roasting and smoking and his childhood in Congo flows freely through his cuisine. This distinctive hallmark is further enriched by a unique use of vinegars (over 400 house creations) that add depth and complexity to his jus. The resulting lineup of some forty small dishes, a truly herculean task, is surgically crafted and often visually striking: cuttlefish steamed in sake, puffed barley, briny seasoning and seaweed; watercress, smoked beetroot, langoustine milk, iced burnt langoustine heads. Each meal is a feat of culinary excellence, supervised by a diffident, yet single-minded chef, whose watchful eye never leaves the serving hatch.

AM is the creation of Alexandre Mazzia. The restaurant is tiny, with a kitchen so small that many domestic kitchens are bigger. It contains 24 seats of which there are 5 counter seats where you basically sit right on the pass.  
Mr. Mazzia was born and raised in Congo and claims to be still influenced by his childhood experience.  
The menu is composed differently than in other restaurants as he always has six servings and the diner just chooses how many little plates each serving contains.  
I, of course, went for the jugular and the pricey (€380) biggest menu. This contains 36(!) small dishes grouped together in what seems to be a random order of 6 servings.  
I have been at AM before a few times but for obvious reasons not in the last three years. Mr. Mazzia has not only developed but made a quantum leap. This is so far the meal of the year (276 as of the end of October, not counting any meals in Ryokans where I stayed overnight) and given the advance state of the year, maybe it will stay that way.  
His compositions are bold while at the same time delicate. The layering of flavors is sometimes subtle and sometimes aggressive. The execution is always flawless, which is a feat when serving 36 dishes. But most of all the combination of ingredients and thus flavors is simply stunning. Dishes like Goosefoot in tempura-vodka, pike roe, chili, shell dust or Seaweed, sweet potato ointment, licorice, bottarga sound like they could never work but they do. Very much so.  
Mr. Mazzia does all of this in a tiny little kitchen with three other cooks while chatting with the regulars who are of course placed at the counter. To churn out such a kitchen this number of dishes for 24 dinners is by itself an accomplishment, but doing it at such a quality and tastiness beggars belief.  
The front of the house is very competent in being able to explain all dishes. It is quite an accomplishment to be able to announce what must be hundreds of ingredients during the whole meal.  
There is a champagne, wine, sake and non alcoholic pairing available which gets managed by the team flexible depending on the speed of drinking.  
All together this makes not for a good, but a great meal. I cannot wait to go back. It truly is worth a journey.  

[Less](#)

Slow cooked cod loin is covered in a light, airy garlic mayonnaise sauce and comes with an incredible ‘persillade’ sauce (garlic, parsley and shallots).

urls.title: [France: Marseille] An Exhausting Meal at 3* AM Par Alexandre Mazzia
urls.content:
**Rating: 1/5**

The reviews for 3\* **AM Par Alexandre Mazzia** are polarizing among my foodie friends - either you love it or hate it. When I’m thinking of how to describe my meal there, one word comes to mind and that is “exhausting”. Alas this is not the start of a glowing review but let’s back up to the beginning. I had high expectations for this meal given that it was awarded 3 Michelin Stars and “One To Watch” by World’s 50 Best. The chef had grown up in Congo so African influences are abound in his dishes.

The meal started with 8 little bites which was more or less in line with amuse bouches at other restaurants. But then with each course came multiple dishes all at once - sometimes 4, sometimes 6. There were 5 to 6 courses, so in total there were 31 dishes and frankly this format was really exhausting. Now I’ve had meals with more dishes than this but usually they came one at a time. Here, you’re hit with multiple dishes all at once and they don’t even really compliment each other. The other problem was - most of the dishes were pretty unremarkable. Instead of quantity, I wished the focus was on quality. The lack of premium ingredients here also fails to justify the high price tag. The only substantial protein that we had was the crawfish and that was it.

The course that I had the most issues with was the last savory one which was supposedly Asian-inspired. A lot of the flavors were not well-balanced at all. There was a bread with a spicy butter which was so pungent, it tasted like eating untreated Indian spices. Then there was the spinach with beetroot sauce, green beans with cherry, zucchini flower with passionfruit (so sweet, it tasted more like a dessert), a tempura that was too thick, a broth that tasted like Nissin instant noodles, and wasabi ice cream that was too rich to serve as a palate cleanser. At this time I was still hopeful that desserts would be better. But the same problem persisted - too many little bites that failed to make an impact from a melon ice served in a cup to a weak-tasting matcha.

The service was decent and the atmosphere was casual - there’s nothing wrong with that but then again nothing special about it that would make it a 3\* restaurant. We did notice that the restroom, located in the basement, had an unpleasant cyanobacteria odor and that is unacceptable at a top restaurant.

content.text:
**Rating: 1/5**

The reviews for 3\* **AM Par Alexandre Mazzia** are polarizing among my foodie friends - either you love it or hate it. When I’m thinking of how to describe my meal there, one word comes to mind and that is “exhausting”. Alas this is not the start of a glowing review but let’s back up to the beginning. I had high expectations for this meal given that it was awarded 3 Michelin Stars and “One To Watch” by World’s 50 Best. The chef had grown up in Congo so African influences are abound in his dishes.

The meal started with 8 little bites which was more or less in line with amuse bouches at other restaurants. But then with each course came multiple dishes all at once - sometimes 4, sometimes 6. There were 5 to 6 courses, so in total there were 31 dishes and frankly this format was really exhausting. Now I’ve had meals with more dishes than this but usually they came one at a time. Here, you’re hit with multiple dishes all at once and they don’t even really compliment each other. The other problem was - most of the dishes were pretty unremarkable. Instead of quantity, I wished the focus was on quality. The lack of premium ingredients here also fails to justify the high price tag. The only substantial protein that we had was the crawfish and that was it.

The course that I had the most issues with was the last savory one which was supposedly Asian-inspired. A lot of the flavors were not well-balanced at all. There was a bread with a spicy butter which was so pungent, it tasted like eating untreated Indian spices. Then there was the spinach with beetroot sauce, green beans with cherry, zucchini flower with passionfruit (so sweet, it tasted more like a dessert), a tempura that was too thick, a broth that tasted like Nissin instant noodles, and wasabi ice cream that was too rich to serve as a palate cleanser. At this time I was still hopeful that desserts would be better. But the same problem persisted - too many little bites that failed to make an impact from a melon ice served in a cup to a weak-tasting matcha.

The service was decent and the atmosphere was casual - there’s nothing wrong with that but then again nothing special about it that would make it a 3\* restaurant. We did notice that the restroom, located in the basement, had an unpleasant cyanobacteria odor and that is unacceptable at a top restaurant.

# Marseille’s Michelin-Starred Restaurants — Research Brief

## TL;DR

Marseille is home to **two three-Michelin-star restaurants** in the 2026 guide: **AM par Alexandre Mazzia**, a tiny, avant-garde venue where Congolese childhood memories are translated into dozens of boldly spiced micro-dishes; and **Le Petit Nice Passedat**, a seafood institution overlooking the Mediterranean. The city also boasts a growing constellation of one-star kitchens (e.g., Belle de Mars, Sépia, Une Table au Sud) that have turned the port city into a genuine fine-dining destination post-COVID. AM is the most talked-about table for adventurous gastronomes, but its high price and polarizing “flood-of-bites” format draw mixed reactions.

---

## Overview: Marseille’s Starred Scene

Historically overlooked by Paris-centric gastronomes, Marseille has undergone a “huge post-covid shift” and is now a culinary hotspot ([Paris Eater](https://www.pariseater.com/france-travel-guides/michelin-star-restaurants-in-marseille/)). The 2026 MICHELIN Guide France lists **12 starred restaurants** in Marseille and its immediate surroundings, including:

| Stars | Representative Restaurants |
|-------|---------------------------|
| **3★** | AM par Alexandre Mazzia; Le Petit Nice Passedat |
| **1★** | Belle de Mars, Sépia, Ourea, Les Bords de Mer, Peron, Une Table au Sud, Signature (Coline Faulquier), Ekume, Kin, etc. |

([Michelin Guide](https://guide.michelin.com/us/en/provence-alpes-cote-dazur/marseille/restaurants/all-starred); [Michelin Guide France 2026 full list](https://guide.michelin.com/us/en/article/news-and-views/michelin-star-restaurants-france-full-list))

---

## Deep Dive: AM par Alexandre Mazzia

### What It Is

**AM par Alexandre Mazzia** is a 24-seat restaurant in a quiet residential street near Marseille’s Stade Vélodrome. It holds **three Michelin stars** as of the 2026 guide and is ranked among the world’s most inventive tables. The dining room is spare, the kitchen is tiny (“many domestic kitchens are bigger”), and the chef’s personality dominates every aspect of the experience ([Foodle](https://foodle.pro/restaurants/marseille/am-par-alexandre-mazzia-1); [Michelin Guide](https://guide.michelin.com/qa/en/provence-alpes-cote-dazur/marseille/restaurant/am-par-alexandre-mazzia)).

### Chef Background

- **Alexandre Mazzia** was born in 1976 in **Pointe-Noire, Republic of the Congo**, where he lived until age 15. His childhood was shaped by the Atlantic coast, grilled fish, smoked papaya, tapioca, and above all **spices and chili**—what he calls the “spine” of his cooking ([Arab News FR](https://www.arabnews.fr/node/52511/culture); [L’Express](http://lexpress.fr/styles/plaisirs/saveurs/top-chef-2020-7-choses-a-savoir-sur-alexandre-mazzia-le-fada-des-fourneaux_2124075.html)).
- He moved to France at 16, trained at the École Hôtelière Santos Dumont, and was a **professional basketball player** before committing fully to cooking ([Reporter Gourmet](https://reportergourmet.com/en/news/8096-alexandre-mazzia-from-basketball-player-to-3-star-michelin-chef-the-gourmet-anarchy-of-an-outsider)).
- He opened AM in 2014; Gault & Millau named him **Cuisinier de l’Année 2019** while the restaurant still held one star. It has since climbed to three.

### Cuisine & Philosophy

Mazzia’s self-described through-lines are **“spiced, roasted, smoked.”** The menu is not a traditional sequence of appetizer-main-dessert but a **“voyage”** made up of **multiple sequences**, each containing several small plates served at once.

Key technical signatures:
- **Vinegars**: over 400 house-made vinegars are used to deepen jus and sauces ([Michelin Guide](https://guide.michelin.com/qa/en/provence-alpes-cote-dazur/marseille/restaurant/am-par-alexandre-mazzia)).
- **African-Provencal fusion**: Dishes such as *chocolate filled with smoked eel and chili*, *banana-coffee-hibiscus*, and *seaweed popcorn* fuse his Congolese memories with Mediterranean produce ([Foodle](https://foodle.pro/restaurants/marseille/am-par-alexandre-mazzia-1); [Tendance Ouest](https://www.tendanceouest.com/actualite-298723-alexandre-mazzia-le-chef-qui-pimente-les-saveurs-d-enfance.html)).
- **Surgical precision**: The Michelin Guide notes the lineup of some **forty small dishes** is “surgically crafted and often visually striking” ([Michelin Guide](https://guide.michelin.com/qa/en/provence-alpes-cote-dazur/marseille/restaurant/am-par-alexandre-mazzia)).

### The Experience

- **Seating**: 24 covers, including **5 counter seats** directly on the pass where Mazzia interacts with regulars ([Foodle review](https://foodle.pro/restaurants/marseille/am-par-alexandre-mazzia-1)).
- **Format**: Diners choose how many sequences they want (commonly 3 to 5 sequences). A maximal “Grand Voyage” can involve **30+ individual bites** across six servings. This format is deliberately intense and unconventional.
- **Beverage pairings**: Champagne, wine, sake, and non-alcoholic pairings are available, paced flexibly by the front-of-house team ([Foodle review](https://foodle.pro/restaurants/marseille/am-par-alexandre-mazzia-1)).

### Pricing (Indicative)

Prices are steep and have risen with each star promotion. As of early 2026, third-party menu aggregators list:

- **Premier Pas / lunch entry**: from ~€115–€175
- **Premier voyage** (3 sequences): ~€295
- **Allons plus loin** (4 sequences): ~€395
- **Grand voyage** (5 sequences): ~€435

([TheFork](https://www.thefork.fr/restaurant/am-par-alexandre-mazzia-r829913); [Gault&Millau](https://fr.gaultmillau.com/restaurants/alexandre-mazzia))

The restaurant uses a **€€€€** price symbol in the Michelin Guide, indicating “very expensive.”

### Hours & Booking

- **Open**: Wednesday–Saturday, lunch (12:00–14:00) and dinner (20:00–21:30). Closed Sunday–Tuesday.
- **Reservations**: Highly competitive; book via the restaurant’s own website or phone. The Michelin Guide notes the establishment manages its own bookings ([Michelin Guide](https://guide.michelin.com/qa/en/provence-alpes-cote-dazur/marseille/restaurant/am-par-alexandre-mazzia); [Official site](https://www.alexandre-mazzia.com/reservation/)).

---

## The Other 3★ Institution: Le Petit Nice Passedat

If AM represents avant-garde disruption, **Le Petit Nice Passedat** is Marseille’s classical temple of seafood. Chef **Gérald Passedat** descends from a family of artists and has run the restaurant—located in a five-star hotel on the Anse de Maldormé—for decades. The cuisine is deeply rooted in the Mediterranean, with a focus on **local rockfish, deep fish sauces, and expert cookery** rather than meat jus. It holds three stars in the 2026 guide and offers panoramic sea views ([Paris Eater](https://www.pariseater.com/france-travel-guides/michelin-star-restaurants-in-marseille/); [Michelin Guide](http://guide.michelin.com/ee/en/provence-alpes-cote-dazur/marseille/restaurant/le-petit-nice); [Foodle](https://foodle.pro/restaurants/marseille/le-petit-nice-passedat)).

**Price point**: Even a three-course lunch menu approaches €200, making it similarly expensive but more traditionally structured ([Paris Eater](https://www.pariseater.com/france-travel-guides/michelin-star-restaurants-in-marseille/)).

---

## Notable 1-Star Alternatives

For diners seeking starred quality at lower intensity or price:

- **Une Table au Sud** (1★): Ludovic Turac’s modern Provencal cooking next to the Old Port; lunch menu around €125. Signature dishes include an airy *aioli* and a refined *bouillabaisse* ([Paris Eater](https://www.pariseater.com/france-travel-guides/michelin-star-restaurants-in-marseille/)).
- **Belle de Mars** (1★): Open-plan kitchen, modern “legible” cuisine by Michel Marini in the 2nd arrondissement ([Michelin Guide](https://guide.michelin.com/tr/en/provence-alpes-cote-dazur/marseille/restaurant/belle-de-mars)).
- **Sépia** (1★): Modern cuisine in the 7th arrondissement; noted for intimate scale ([Michelin Guide](https://guide.michelin.com/en/provence-alpes-cote-dazur/marseille/restaurant/sepia542688)).
- **Signature** (1★): Coline Faulquier (Top Chef finalist, Young Chef Award 2021) offers creative, Mediterranean-anchored author cuisine in the 8th arrondissement ([Signature Marseille](http://signaturemarseille.com/)).

---

## Key Tradeoffs / Controversies

### 1. Polarizing Format: “Exhausting” vs. “Electrifying”

The flood of 30+ small dishes served in batches is divisive.
- **Advocates** call it a “quantum leap” in creativity, praising the “stunning” flavor layering and flawless execution across dozens of bites ([Foodle review](https://foodle.pro/restaurants/marseille/am-par-alexandre-mazzia-1)).
- **Detractors** find the format overwhelming. One 2023 review described the meal as “exhausting,” arguing that quantity overshadows quality, that some combinations are unbalanced (e.g., wasabi ice cream, instant-noodle-like broth), and that the casual atmosphere does not feel “3-star.” The same reviewer noted an **unpleasant odor in the basement restroom**, deeming it unacceptable at this level ([SupertasterMel](http://www.supertastermel.com/2023/01/france-marseille-am-par-alexandre-mazzia.html)).

### 2. Price-to-Value Tension

With flagship menus at **€400+ per person before drinks**, AM sits at the top tier of French dining. Critics argue that the absence of premium proteins (e.g., little beyond crawfish in some menus) makes the price harder to justify compared to traditional luxury ingredients found at other 3-star tables ([SupertasterMel](http://www.supertastermel.com/2023/01/france-marseille-am-par-alexandre-mazzia.html); [Travels for Stars](https://www.travelsforstars.com/blog/2022/05/12/am-par-alexandre-mazzia-marseille)).

### 3. Allergy & Service Slips

A TheFork reviewer (2025) praised the experience but flagged **two service faults**: an allergy was not fully respected until a mid-meal correction, and an allergen appeared in the dessert course. They concluded it was nonetheless a “très beau moment,” suggesting the kitchen’s ambition sometimes outpaces operational rigor ([TheFork](https://www.thefork.fr/restaurant/am-par-alexandre-mazzia-r829913)).

### 4. Accessibility

- **Location**: AM is outside the touristic Vieux-Port zone, in a residential neighborhood near the stadium.
- **Hours**: Only four days a week (Wed–Sat) and closed for long stretches of the year (common for top French restaurants, but still a logistical hurdle).
- **Booking**: The tiny size means reservations are scarce; some diners report needing to book well in advance.

---

## Comparison: AM vs. Le Petit Nice Passedat

| Dimension | AM par Alexandre Mazzia | Le Petit Nice Passedat |
|-----------|------------------------|------------------------|
| **Style** | Avant-garde, micro-dishes, Congolese-Provencal fusion | Classic fine dining, Mediterranean seafood |
| **Setting** | Residential street, tiny 24-seat room, open kitchen | Seafront 5-star hotel, panoramic views |
| **Chef Persona** | Former athlete, self-taught creative, “artist-cum-chef” | Third-generation artist family, seafood traditionalist |
| **Menu Structure** | 3–5 “sequences” totaling 20–40+ bites | Conventional courses, focus on fish and sauces |
| **Price** | ~€115–€435+ | ~€200+ (lunch) to much higher for tasting menus |
| **Vibe** | Casual, intense, “gourmet anarchy” | Formal, serene, Mediterranean luxury |

([Michelin Guide](https://guide.michelin.com/qa/en/provence-alpes-cote-dazur/marseille/restaurant/am-par-alexandre-mazzia); [Paris Eater](https://www.pariseater.com/france-travel-guides/michelin-star-restaurants-in-marseille/))

---

## Practical Information

**AM par Alexandre Mazzia**
- **Address**: 9 rue François-Rocca, 13008 Marseille, France
- **Phone**: +33 4 91 24 83 63
- **Email**: contact@alexandre-mazzia.com
- **Website**: [alexandre-mazzia.com](https://www.alexandre-mazzia.com)
- **Reservations**: Online via official site; phone recommended for same-day requests

**Le Petit Nice Passedat**
- **Address**: 17 Rue des Braves / Anse de Maldormé, 13007 Marseille
- **Phone**: +33 4 91 59 25 92
- **Website**: [passedat.fr](https://www.passedat.fr/fr/le-petit-nice/restaurant-3-etoiles-marseille-bord-de-mer)

---

## Resources & References

- [AM par Alexandre Mazzia – Michelin Guide](https://guide.michelin.com/qa/en/provence-alpes-cote-dazur/marseille/restaurant/am-par-alexandre-mazzia) — Three-star citation and culinary description.
- [AM par Alexandre Mazzia – Foodle](https://foodle.pro/restaurants/marseille/am-par-alexandre-mazzia-1) — User reviews, sample dishes, and pricing history.
- [The Top Michelin Star Restaurants in Marseille – Paris Eater](https://www.pariseater.com/france-travel-guides/michelin-star-restaurants-in-marseille/) — English-language guide to Marseille’s starred scene.
- [Alexandre Mazzia: le chef qui embarque avec des goûts d’enfance pimentés – Arab News FR](https://www.arabnews.fr/node/52511/culture) — Profile of Mazzia’s Congolese childhood and culinary influences.
- [Alexandre Mazzia – L’Express](http://lexpress.fr/styles/plaisirs/saveurs/top-chef-2020-7-choses-a-savoir-sur-alexandre-mazzia-le-fada-des-fourneaux_2124075.html) — 7 key facts about the chef.
- [Alexandre Mazzia – Reporter Gourmet](https://reportergourmet.com/en/news/8096-alexandre-mazzia-from-basketball-player-to-3-star-michelin-chef-the-gourmet-anarchy-of-an-outsider) — Career trajectory from basketball to three stars.
- [Le Petit Nice – Michelin Guide](http://guide.michelin.com/ee/en/provence-alpes-cote-dazur/marseille/restaurant/le-petit-nice) — Three-star citation for Gérald Passedat.
- [Marseille All Starred Restaurants – Michelin Guide](https://guide.michelin.com/us/en/provence-alpes-cote-dazur/marseille/restaurants/all-starred) — Official 2026 starred list for Marseille.
- [An Exhausting Meal at 3* AM – SupertasterMel](http://www.supertastermel.com/2023/01/france-marseille-am-par-alexandre-mazzia.html) — Critical 1/5 review detailing format and service issues.
- [AM par Alexandre Mazzia – TheFork](https://www.thefork.fr/restaurant/am-par-alexandre-mazzia-r829913) — Aggregated diner reviews and menu pricing.
- [Signature Marseille – Coline Faulquier](http://signaturemarseille.com/) — One-star alternative in the 8th arrondissement.

---

## Raw Notes

- **2026 Guide update**: France & Monaco saw 54 new one-stars, 7 new two-stars, and 1 new three-star nationally; AM and Le Petit Nice retained their three stars in Marseille ([Michelin.com](https://www.michelin.com/en/publications/products-and-services/new-selection-michelin-guide-france-monaco-2026)).
- **Vinegar obsession**: Mazzia’s use of >400 house vinegars is one of the highest counts reported in any Michelin-starred kitchen globally.
- **Kitchen size**: Four cooks (including Mazzia) serve 24 diners with 30+ distinct preparations per maximal menu—an exceptionally high dish-to-cook ratio.
- **Food truck fire (April 2026)**: A news item mentions a Marseille food truck linked to a “famous three-star chef” being destroyed by flames; while not directly about the restaurant, it underscores local media interest in the city’s top chefs ([Gala.fr](https://www.gala.fr/l_actu/news_de_stars/a-marseille-le-food-truck-d-un-celebre-chef-trois-etoiles-au-guide-michelin-devaste-par-les-flammes-ce-que-l-on-sait-20260409)).
